Course Description

Introduction to system support for application programs, both on a single node

and over a network, including OS application interface, inter-process

communication, introduction to systems and network programming, and simple

computer security concepts.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 221 with a grade of C or better; grade of C or

better in CSCE 312 or concurrent enrollment in CSCE 350/ECEN 350 or

ECEN 350/CSCE 350.

Special Course Designation

None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Understand what system software is involved for an application program to

run, both on a single node and over a network, and how this system software

is to be used.

Perform system-level programming.

Use system components, such as memory, ﬁle systems, process control, inter-

process communication, and networking.

Understand problems and pitfalls encountered in the design and

implementation of multithreaded and networked applications and systems.

Use services provided by the underlying operating system by programming

directly at the operating system interface level.

Understand the following aspects of a computer system:

What is an operating system?

What are the components of an operating system?

What are system calls and their purpose?

Execution of a program; function calls; interrupts.

The OS application interface, ﬁle system, and process control.

Inter-process communication

Concurrency, Threads, Process/Thread Synchronization

Understand the following aspects of a computer system:

What is an operating system?

What are the components of an operating system?

What are system calls and their purpose?

Execution of a program; function calls; interrupts.

The OS application interface, ﬁle system, and process control.

Inter-process communication

Concurrency, Threads, Process/Thread Synchronization

Network Socket Programming

Parallel and Distributed Computing

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces

URL for Resource:  https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~remzi/OSTEP/

This material Is: Recommended

Beej's Guide to Interprocess Communication

URL for Resource:  https://beej.us/guide/bgipc/html/split/

Grades for all the assignments will be released on  Canvas.

A student can request regrading of any graded material if the student believes that

the points assigned are inconsistent with the quality and merits of the submitted

work. To request regrading, the student needs to follow the guidelines below:

Regrade requests must be submitted to the instructor  within one week  after

the graded item is returned to the student. After this time limit, no regrade

requests will be honored.

Regrade requests must be submitted electronically through email to the

course instructor or TA, with reasonable justiﬁcation and documentation.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Students can submit work late.

The lab is due  by 11:59 PM on the due date .

If students have diﬃculties ﬁnishing an assignment, contact the instructor

before  the deadline.

If you have an excused absence, you can get a due date extension.

Ensure you have emailed the documentation of an excused

absence to the instructor

Assignments submitted late will be decreased by 10% once it is late and

an additional 10% per day or fraction of a class after that.

AI Statement

With the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, programming and problem-

solving practices are evolving. While these tools can assist with brainstorming,

debugging, or exploring alternative approaches, they  do not replace the essential

skills of designing, writing, and testing your own code . Nor do they substitute for

the critical thinking required to analyze problems, evaluate solutions, and apply

course concepts independently.

In this course, you are permitted to use generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, GitHub

Copilot, Google Gemini) to support your learning, under the following conditions:

AI may be used as a supplement, not a substitute.  You may consult AI for

hints, debugging suggestions, or explanations, but you are expected to write

and understand your own code.

You must cite AI assistance.  If AI tools inform your work (e.g., generating a

code snippet, suggesting an algorithm, or helping you debug), you must

clearly document this in comments within your code (e.g.,  # AI tool

(ChatGPT) suggested this loop structure   ).

AI should not complete your assignments for you.  Submitting work

primarily written by AI is considered academic dishonesty. You are responsible

for understanding and being able to explain every line of code you submit.

Limit reliance.  No more than 30% of an assignment’s code may be directly

AI-generated. The majority of the work must reflect your own effort and

understanding.

Important reminders:

Generative AI may produce incorrect or inefficient code. Always test and verify

any AI-assisted solution before using it.

AI tools may generate code that does not follow best practices or course

requirements. You are responsible for ensuring your work meets assignment

expectations.

Misrepresentation of AI-generated work as your own will be reported to the

Aggie Honor System Office . The minimum penalties include:

A grade of 0 on the assignment

A grade of F* in the course

Honor Violation Probation
